title: Professional Email Writing
definition: Professional emails follow a clear structure: greeting, purpose, details, action requested, and a polite closing.
rules: Start with a greeting: Hi [Name], / Dear [Name],
State the purpose clearly in the first line: I am writing to follow up on...
Keep the body short and use bullet points for multiple items.
End with a clear call to action: Please let me know if you have any questions.
Close politely: Best regards, / Thanks and regards,
examples:
Subject: Follow-up on Project Timeline

Hi Team,

I hope you're doing well. I wanted to follow up on the project timeline we discussed yesterday. Could you please confirm the revised deadline by tomorrow?

Thanks and regards,
Ananya

Subject: Request for Leave Approval

Dear Manager,

I would like to request leave from 10th to 12th July for a personal commitment. I will complete my pending tasks before I leave. Kindly approve at your convenience.

Best regards,
Ravi

commonMistakes.right: Please let me know if this works, or please proceed with the next steps.
commonMistakes.why: 'Do the needful' is considered outdated/unclear in modern global business English; be specific about the action requested.

commonMistakes.right: Please get back to me on this.
commonMistakes.why: 'Revert' alone means 'to return to a previous state'; use 'get back to me' or 'reply to me' instead.

commonMistakes.wrong: kindly send me the file asap.
commonMistakes.right: Could you please send me the file at your earliest convenience?
commonMistakes.why: Start with a capital letter, and prefer a fuller polite phrase over the abbreviation 'asap' in formal emails.

quiz.question: Which closing is most appropriate for a professional email?
quiz.explanation: 'Best regards,' is a standard professional closing.

quiz.question: Which phrase is clearer in professional emails?
options: Please review and confirm by Friday.
quiz.answer: Please review and confirm by Friday.
quiz.explanation: Specific, actionable requests are clearer than vague phrases.

quiz.sentence: Please revert me with the update.
quiz.correction: Please get back to me with the update.
quiz.explanation: Use 'get back to me', not 'revert me', in modern professional English.
